speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewer, Rebecca [? Zemen ?] [? Bonconi ?], tape six. Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the on the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Matteo. He lived till around 1938. He live-- he lived in Rhode Island. And he went to Israel. But going-- during the war, he was an English soldier. You know. He became-- he went to the army, with Mr. [? Leon ?] [? Mayer. ?] They were together. But he came back and-- to Israel again. And he got married. He got three daughters, all married there. And-- but he died. He died seven years ago, young. He had a heart attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to see you when you arrived in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never saw him, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've never seen him since?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1938, they left, the two brothers. And I supposed to go to Israel, myself. But I didn't go. I never saw them. The two of them died there. The other one, I haven't got the picture with this. But I saw the one daughter, you see. She came to see me in Rhodesia. If you don't find [INAUDIBLE] it's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this picture here, who it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the wife of my late brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the three daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Matteo.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you give me the people's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Varda. Varda. Mazal, and [? Tika. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the wife is Victoria. Victoria, no, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flora.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, Flora. The name is Flora.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever seen them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the one. The-- the one daughter came to see me, before she got married, to-- to Rhodesia. She was with me one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very close to your brother, Matteo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I never saw them from the time. You see. And I was sorry he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you ever go to Israel to see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was helping my husband, always, in the shop. And he-- and I was very nervous to fly. You see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're scared of airplanes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't like to go alone. But he went to see them. And I-- I was in the shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you find that because of the concentration camp you are very scared of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was always afraid to fly. You know. And then I learned, even now, I'm afraid to go and see the family. I get too nervous in the airplane.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're not generally a nervous person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually forgot quite quickly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You blocked it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. Are you ready? Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the person on the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brother Maurizio. He was also in the concentration camp, when he was liberated, by the Americans, in April, before me two moths, April, May. One month before me, he was liberated. And he went to Italy, in Rome-- to Rome, you know? And then there, he find a-- a girlfriend, And they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Lina. ?] But she died also. He died first. After [? 30 ?] years, she died. But she-- they lived two children. One is [? Benjamino, ?] Benjamin, who lives in Rhode Island, in America. And the other one is Andre, who lives in Rome. And I never saw them, the two children, again. Because my nephew, when I brought my brother to Rhodesia, it was from 1950, perhaps in 1962. And then they left Rhodesia. I never saw them again, you see. So he died. He died 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bronchitis, they said, a flu.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother, Matteo?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a heart attack. Yeah. And that's it. I didn't see. And then the wife, she-- we used to write letters. And all of a sudden, she got sick. I think she had cancer, the wife. And she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where are the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they still in contact with the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- they-- [? Benjamino, ?] he-- he even wrote once a letter, a card. And then he went to see my son in America. Because it's no so far. And then when Jeanette went holiday, he went to see them. He's really-- he's married happily, and got one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The name of the child? What is the name of the child, Jeanette?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin, BB, BB's son, what is the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife is [? Robyn. ?] His wife is [? Robyn. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The wife is [? Robyn. ?] And.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see if I have it written down.

speaker: interviewer
text: You will notice that the name, Sciarcon, is spelt differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sciarcon in Italian is Sciarcon. They put a C, I.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's spelled differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But Sciarcon is the best, because they used to call, Sciarcon, you know, Lucia Sciarcon, you know how it is. So we have to do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he the youngest brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they're a lot young.

speaker: interviewer
text: The youngest? So you went right through the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was in Buchenwald. He was with their father, [? Nola. ?] The camp, you know, he survived [INAUDIBLE]. He had a, you know, a tea room in Italy, in Rome. But they used to close 2 o'clock, 3 o'clock, they used to play little things. He used to sell cigarettes. He opened a-- but they were working very hard there in Italy, I think. He-- he died from the bronchitis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a year, I came here in 1978. I came here, 1979 he died.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he used to have rheumatic fever when he was young, Mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle Maurizio. Did he have rheumatic fever?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was young, before he went to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he suffered.

speaker: interviewee
text: He suffered. Yeah, he was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's amazing that he survived the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 13, 14 when he had-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's amazing that he survived the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because he had rheumatic fever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this picture is Maurizio, with the family but-- when they came to Rhodesia. You see. And after they-- they had a baby, but it's not yet. Now he's a grown up. He was born 1962, the other one. You see, he was seven when they came to Rhodesia, BB.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many children have they got?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two-- who sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: BB, who's that one, Benjamin. And the other one is Andre, who lives in Rome. So he's not in that picture, because he was a baby when he lived in Rhodesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you've got two sisters, who married Italians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, those two sisters that married Italian--

speaker: interviewee
text: The one-- the one that lives, this is Djoya. You saw Djoya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all. And then Rina she died. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Now, those two sisters that have married Italian, did they bring up their children in a Jewish faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They're Italian.

speaker: interviewer
text: They don't-- they don't practice Jewish religion.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this brother? Did they practice Jewish religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The one son. The other one is in Italy, I don't know, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they do bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes. He was in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he did his bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had a [INAUDIBLE]. Yes. And they did [INAUDIBLE], the two of them, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's nice. So it's only your sister's children that don't practice. But do-- what do the children say about it? They don't say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do you they know that you survived the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my daughter-- my sister-in-law told me, when they grow up, they can choose what they like.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ones, yes. Well, that's normal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he choose the Jewish-- he married a Jewish girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He has baby, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rhode Island, he lives in Rhode Island. Very clever boy. He came when he was seven. He left when he was 14. And then he saw-- he-- he was the first of the class, every-- in every class. And then they went to Rome. And then he was working for the agencies. You know? And he met a girl there. And then he got married and went to America. So he got one son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lovely. Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my sister Djoya.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture here?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this picture taken before or after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, after the war. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she go to the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She came-- she survived. She's still alive. This is the one, she had a baby girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute. We haven't filmed the baby girl yet. Can you just tell me, is she's younger or older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is Djoya again, with the daughter, Rosemarie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Here is when she came out already. She was married there. And here she had a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosemarie. Rose had my mother's name. And-- and this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we haven't filmed that one yet. Can you just talk a little bit about her and the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosemarie, she's married. She got a husband and one son. Her-- her name-- the son's name is David, the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the baby's already married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- yeah, she was already married, yes. She's like-- she's about 40, 42.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because she had-- she didn't have children straight away. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So she only had-- your sister only had one child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one daughter. One daughter, her name is Rosemarie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- what was her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aldo. He's here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Drovandi.

speaker: interviewer
text: She met him in Italy after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She-- she-- he was from Rhodos. They knew each other from Rhodos, and when my-- when my sister came back from-- I don't know. She found the name in the survivor. And he came to look after in Rome! From Genoa, he's from Genoa. He came to look after her and say he wants to marry Djoya.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mean the birth-- he was-- during the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was a military there, during the war, in Rhodes Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then your sister went to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They met-- they met there. And then he left. We left to the concentration camp. And then, all of a sudden, he just came to [? Santa ?] [? Paulo ?] where we've been. He say he saw the name of Djoya, Lucy. And he came there and to look for my sister. So they got married. Then they had this child, only one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he's not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he's not Jewish. So they were very happy. But we went to see them in 1962. We were there for a month. They were happy. And [INAUDIBLE] they always said that he had bronchitis. I don't know what he died, very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now the daughter, she buried, she got this son. Whose name is [? Davico, ?] David. But the-- the husband of my niece is very sick. He-- he got also-- arthritis. He doesn't move. He's in bed. So the mother, shame, she got the son of 20. His son, of my-- of Rosemarie, is 20. But her husband is sick in bed. And always when my sister talk to me, she say well, they come to wash him, you know, every day. Cost a lot of money there in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she works, you know. She works. My niece works. So my sister, she used to cook for them. Because her husband is sick. And when she comes at least she can find something. But now she was sick, because she is 82, 83, my sister. You see. And then she's in the flat alone now. She says they phone each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- just film this one now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia, [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my sister, Djoya, who we saw before. Now she's old. You know. And this is with the late husband. He died very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we're going to show the late husband just now. We're only filming one photograph at a time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, I'm sorry. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your sister that went with you to the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Djoya. I used to look after, because I was afraid. Because she was the oldest, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was like your mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like my mom. And I used to look after her. So we went-- we were already together in the same camp. And we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came to get married, she remained in Italy, with the other sister. And then got married also in the same year. The three of us, Maurizio, and Djoya, and myself. We-- we met in 1947. So she got only the one daughter and one grandson. And she lives in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is my brother-in-law, Aldo Drovandi. He-- he was very nice fellow. And when I went to Italy in 1962, he was-- he was well, and all of a sudden he got sick. And he died. Maybe 20 years, so my sister, she's a widower.

speaker: interviewer
text: They only died 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 years ago, he died, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he survived by wife and one daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents in Rhodes Island, did they meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They never met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were already dead [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a beautiful picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my wedding. I married nearly 49 years. 1947 I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married in Salisbury, but they got [INAUDIBLE], you see? Because my husband used to work there, and they had a hall. And then-- and after, they didn't have-- it was on Sunday, they had no photographers, and things like that, like now. So after we got married, we went to Salisbury. We dress up again to make the picture. And then we went for the honeymoon to [INAUDIBLE]. And there we met a lot of people. They were in the [INAUDIBLE], you know, in the-- because it was in-- in-- when we got married, it was June. It was very cold in Rhodesia. That time it was winter. So we had a nice time there. And that night when we arrived, they-- they killed a-- a leopard, you know, and they show us the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: A leopard?

speaker: interviewee
text: A leopard, yes. And then after, from the honeymoon, we came back. And we went to live in [INAUDIBLE] for-- I lived there from 1947 till 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had quite a good life with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very happy, and very good, for-- to me and to my family. He used to help all my family in Italy, sending parcels and money. Every month he used to send them, to my sister, to me, and materials, and shirts, and trousers, always. To send to my nephews, and to my brother-in-law. You know? He was a very good husband. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. And I miss him very much. He even got nobody also in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of nightmares [? and that ?] when you-- when you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I got married I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of the concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle-- middle of the night, I used to shout, and cry, and he said, don't worry, you are here. You are with me. Lucy, you are with me, don't worry. I said, ooh, I dreamed the Germans they were catching me or want to kill me. But no, it's-- it's just, you're dreaming. And then after, I was a little bit better every time. And then I got [? over. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't get sick, or depression, or anything of that sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived quite a happy life afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: A normal life.

speaker: interviewee
text: A normal life. And I used to help my husband in the shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right through, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia, this is a beautiful photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is also taken in Zimbabwe, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Zimbabwe, yes, in Salisbury. It was a holiday day, you know, and then we went to the-- we made an appointment. So we went to have this picture. Jeanette, she was one year old. And Albert was two and a half, nearly three. Two and a half old. And my husband and myself. Look, I look thin there, eh? And this is a nice, what do you say? Nice memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm so happy. I've got this together. We had no chance, you know, to-- to be with-- would like [INAUDIBLE]. We had in the wedding for Albert. But we did the picture, the family, I don't know why, when we got married in Johannesburg. So what do you want? What am I say?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture with Albert, and Jeanette, and myself. He came to see me in February, because to see me if I was going to have the operation for the-- heart operation. You see. But still, I'm thinking if I do or not. And that's why I'm showing this picture, because he's not here with the family. So I wanted to see, this is my son. He's a nice, and caring, and lovely, he-- he loves us too much, me and Jeanette.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a lovely picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I miss him very much. And the wife, she was in Johannesburg. But she couldn't come and see me, because she had a bat mitzvah from his-- her family, you know. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does he do in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he's in fashions, you know. They've got factories to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Printing and-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he happy there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very happy there, already 17 years there. And the children are, one is finished the university. And the other one, she's still there in Santa Barbara.

speaker: interviewer
text: One, two, three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is my grandchildren. They-- the first day when they came from America, last year. They went to the beach. And they burned themselves. They were so sick. And they were going out for dinner, they couldn't open even the eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this one here is their firstborn, is Nicole. And the other one is Gina, and Jeanette, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the center.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the center is my daughter. And they were so sick that night. I-- I-- I was so upset to see them so burned here, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they write to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had fever. They like me very much. They say I love you, to granny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they write to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they don't write. Only on the telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Telephone.

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't write these days, you see. But when it's their-- their birthdays, I phone them. You see. And then we talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What wonderful pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my-- my two granddaughters. You see. This is Nicole. She was born in 1973, on the 9th of May.

speaker: interviewee
text: March.

speaker: interviewee
text: March, sorry. March 19, '73.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's your son's daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son daughter, yeah. And she was in the university, in Santa Barbara. Now she finished. And she's doing, what is she doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Communication, PR.

speaker: interviewee
text: Communication.

speaker: interviewee
text: One is doing, it's called, I think, communications, but in the film industry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Madame Lucia?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Gina. This is the second daughter of my son--

speaker: interviewer
text: The youngest.

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest. And is still-- she's in the university in Santa Barbara. She's 21. No, she's not. She-- she-- she'll be this year, the end of the year. Because she was born in December, 16th of December. And she's still in the university. Sometimes she says, hi, Granny. How are you doing? In America. You know? So I miss them very much. And I-- I hope they have a nice life, and good mazels.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my auntie with my daughter, Jeanette. She--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your auntie's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flora.

speaker: interviewer
text: Flora who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flora Tarika. She was [INAUDIBLE] before. And then this is my daughter. She went to America to see her. Because she lived long ago, Rhodes Island. And she married. She was already married. She married in America. And she's 92 years old. She's the sister of my mother. Only survivor in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she didn't go to the camp? She went to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She got one daughter and two grandchildren. So Jeanette went to America, and she was stayed with her for a few weeks. Because she loved it. And she said the house is open. You will send her here. And I can't send her here, because I need-- I need Jeanette here. You know. And she's very nice. And they care all the time. She used to write letters. Now she-- she's getting old. She says she can't write. So she phones sometimes. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she older than your mother, or younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She-- she was younger than my mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, before, yeah. Yes. Now she lives in America. I think she's alone at home. She's got a companion and-- to look after her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She came to attend the wedding?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I forgot to tell you about, I saw her the last time I saw her. It was 1972, when Albert got married. She came to the wedding with her husband. And they-- they stayed with me six weeks. They supposed to come to the wedding of [? Yvette. ?] But because my uncle couldn't make it to come to Cape Town. So they didn't come. And after six weeks, they left for America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were in Johannesburg with you at the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's nice. Were you very emotional when you saw her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was like it was my mother. You know, to see [INAUDIBLE]. To come for the wedding, for the two weddings they came. But she couldn't make it, the other one. And she was so happy. Always she said, I never forget what nice people you were. We-- we look after themselves, you know, for the six weeks they were there. Sorry, she said, I gave you trouble because uncle was sick. What can you do? That's it. This is a picture of my family. Albert with the wife, and Jeanette on top. And then it's Nicole, Gina, and myself. Because they are not here, so I have to show the people, my family. And what other nieces in Italy, and-- and a sister in Italy. Sorry they are not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do your grandchildren know that you were a survivor of the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They know. And I showed the number, even when they came this time. And I-- she say, ooh. Granny, we saw that picture, what do you call? And we were crying. And even my son, he said, Mommy, what you were telling us when we were young, it was the same story? So they're proud that I'm alive. And that's all. And then they were happy to see me. When they were leaving, they were crying. They say, you must come to America. So I hope one day that I see them again, please God.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I think you're scared to go by airplane.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same as my dad. That's his big problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise, I can go. They said to me, come to [INAUDIBLE]. Better as I can go to Italy and see my nieces there. To go to [INAUDIBLE] is the same thing. Once you are in the plane. So this is my happy family. And I miss them very much. Always I worry, how they are. Sometimes I say to [INAUDIBLE], I must phone Albert, or to say, how is he. You know. But he phones me always. Because I said in America, it's cheaper than here-- than here. It costs me a lot of money the-- the telephone. So, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they-- when they all see the video one day, they will know all about you and the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll-- you'll be happy, Albert, and Jeanette, and Nicole, and Gina to see this picture, when one day, I'm not there. And you say, poor granny, she suffered too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for allowing us to video you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much for coming. And sorry the trouble. What kind of message?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to give a message to them, any message? Because you are going to be sending them the video one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS] I don't know what say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, whatever you feel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever you have in your heart to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I didn't say it, but Jackie's name. I'm just repeating again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter-in-law is Jackie. And she got a family in Johannesburg. And they're very nice people, the mother and father, and all her sisters. You know. And then one day, she's going to show this video to the family. Because when I used to tell them, they used to ask me about the concentration camp. And I used to tell them, so and so. She say, ooh. Poor Lucy, you-- how much you suffered. So that's why you must think what-- about your mother-in-law and your mother. And I send you a lovely kiss, to all the family. All right. Bye. [LAUGHS]
